title: What is Domain Shift?
concept: Domain shift happens when the data a model runs on in production differs from the data it was trained on.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is data slicing in model debugging?

options.text: Evaluating performance on specific subsets of data (like weather, lighting, or size)
Cutting raw images into smaller strips before training
Deleting half of the dataset to prevent overfitting

exercises.explain: Slicing allows you to view metrics on specific slices (subgroups) to diagnose localized failures.

cards.title: Leaving the nest
cards.body: A laboratory is clean, controlled, and predictable. The real world is messy, chaotic, and ever-changing. When a vision model is trained inside a clean lab and deployed in the wild, it experiences a shock. This shock is domain shift.

cards.title: Domain Shift
cards.body: Domain Shift (or distribution shift) occurs when the statistical distribution of the input data changes between the training phase (source domain) and the deployment phase (target domain).

cards.title: Driving in Paris
cards.body: Imagine learning to drive in a quiet, sunny suburban neighborhood. You pass your test with 100% accuracy. Suddenly, you are placed in the middle of a chaotic, rainy intersection in Paris. The environment has shifted, and your skills might fail.

cards.title: Hospital X-Ray Shift
cards.body: A pneumonia detector is trained on X-rays from Hospital A. When deployed at Hospital B, its accuracy drops from 95% to 70%. Why? Hospital B uses a different scanner brand, which changes the image contrast and brightness.

cards.title: Val sets don't guarantee robustness
cards.body: You might think a model with a low validation error rate is ready for the world. But if your validation set was cut from the same source domain as your training set, it won't warn you about shifts in the target domain.

cards.title: Source vs. Target Domain
cards.body: How data shifts between training and production:

left.text:
High-quality, labeled images.
Controlled lighting & backgrounds.
Example: Studio product photos.

right.text:
Unseen real-world images.
Varied cameras, noise, weather.
Example: User smartphone uploads.

exercises.prompt: What is the source domain in machine learning?
exercises.explain: The source domain is the training environment/data distribution where the model learns its rules.

options.text: The distribution of data used to train the model
The memory address where the GPU stores weights
The final classification categories of the model
The website where the raw dataset was downloaded

exercises.prompt: True or False: Domain shift can occur even if the classes you want to predict remain exactly the same.
exercises.explain: True. Domain shift changes the appearance of the inputs (e.g., different lighting or camera), not the categories themselves.
A difference in scanner brand represents a domain shift, changing visual pixel properties.
exercises.sentence: When a model is trained on one hospital's scanner and fails on another, it is experiencing a ___ shift.

exercises.prompt: Which of these represents a domain shift scenario?
exercises.explain: Cartoons vs. real photos represents a massive style and texture shift, which is a domain shift.

options.text: Training a cat detector on cartoon drawings and testing it on real photos
Increasing the number of convolutional kernels in the model architecture
Running evaluation on a validation set cut from the training pool

exercises.prompt: You build a hand-written digit classifier that achieves 99% accuracy on clean, black-and-white MNIST images. In production, users upload photos of handwritten digits on whiteboards. The model fails completely. What is the root cause?

options.text: A domain shift between the source domain (clean synthetic images) and the target domain (noisy whiteboard photos).
A class imbalance bug; there are no whiteboard images in the MNIST classes.
The whiteboard photos have too many classes.
The model has too many parameters to process whiteboards.

exercises.explain: Real whiteboard photos have shadows, glares, and different backgrounds compared to clean black-and-white digits, representing a domain shift.
